definition: To *consecrate* is to make a person, place, or object sacred through rite — dedicating it to a god or divine power and setting it apart from ordinary use. In magic it charges an instrument for operation: where to *bless* asks favor upon a thing, to *consecrate* transfers it into the sacred order entirely. Grimoires consecrate the sword, circle, and lamen before any spirit is called.
history: Latin *consecrare* joined *com-* to *sacer*, 'sacred,' meaning 'to make wholly sacred' — to render a thing over to the gods. The turn is that *sacer* was double-edged, naming both the holy and the accursed (whence *execrate*); the Romans knew the sacred was dangerous to touch. Ritual magic inherited the operative sense: a circle must be constructed on cleared, consecrated ground, and grimoires like the *Lesser Key of Solomon* consecrate figures, swords, and equipment before use. Magic: The Gathering keeps the plain sanctifying sense, consecrating ground and relics.

def_sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. lamina (consecration of inscribed plates)
Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic (consecrated circle)
def_sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

commentary: The neat opposition of religion and magic long treated consecration as purely churchly. Keith Thomas unsettled it: the medieval Church deployed a vast repertoire of blessing and consecrating formulae — over holy bread, water, salt, oil — that laypeople valued for a power 'more than merely spiritual or symbolic,' so that the sacramentals shaded into the magical. Frank Klaassen presses from the other side, showing grimoires demanding ground and equipment be 'cleared and consecrated' in language lifted straight from liturgy. The field now stresses this shared ritual vocabulary rather than a policed line between sacrament and spell.

commentary_sources.label: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
commentary_sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

commentary_sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
commentary_sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

etymology.note: The sacer root runs holy AND cursed: 'sacred', 'sacrifice', but also 'execrate'. The Romans knew the sacred was dangerous.
etymology.hook: The sacred root that also means accursed.

flavor_snippets.html:
"The taming and <mark>consecration</mark> of these creatures is evidence of our faith."
—Resimir, Zarichi Temple priest
Once it <mark>consecrated</mark> the dead. Now it desecrates them.
The serpents' dens built into statues of Pharika serve not only to <mark>consecrate</mark> her temples, but as a hidden line of defense.
